edges.description: Loss of AGL debranching activity leaves incompletely degraded glycogen in hepatocytes, driving hepatic storage injury and fibrosis risk.
In GSD IIIa, the same debranching block affects skeletal and cardiac muscle, causing progressive glycogen storage myopathy and cardiomyopathy.
Impaired hepatic glycogen mobilization limits fasting glucose release and clinically manifests as ketotic fasting hypoglycemia.
Impaired hepatic glycogen mobilization lowers blood glucose during fasting.
Recurrent early-life fasting hypoglycemia and hepatic metabolic disease contribute to poor growth.
Hepatic limit-dextrin and glycogen storage enlarges the liver.
Hepatocyte storage injury elevates circulating aminotransferases early in disease.
Hepatic metabolic dysfunction in GSD III is reflected by elevated cholesterol early in life.
Hepatic metabolic dysfunction in GSD III is reflected by elevated triglycerides early in life.
Glucose tetrasaccharide is a glycogen-storage biomarker elevated early in GSD III.
Chronic hepatic glycogen storage injury can progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and related complications.
Skeletal muscle glycogen storage causes progressive weakness and exercise intolerance in GSD IIIa.
Cardiac muscle glycogen storage commonly manifests as left ventricular hypertrophy and cardiomyopathy.
Skeletal muscle involvement is reflected biochemically by persistently elevated creatine kinase.
Skeletal muscle involvement is reflected biochemically by persistently elevated serum creatine kinase.
Lower blood glucose reports impaired hepatic glycogen mobilization during fasting.
Blood glucose monitoring tracks the fasting hypoglycemia branch.
Higher cholesterol tracks hepatic metabolic dysfunction in GSD III.
Higher triglycerides track hepatic metabolic dysfunction in GSD III.
Higher Glc4 reports glycogen storage burden in the hepatic branch.
Higher serum creatine kinase reports the myopathy branch of GSD III.

evidence.reference_title: AGL / glycogen storage disease III (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: AGL | HGNC:321 | glycogen storage disease III | MONDO:0009291 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Frequent meals and cornstarch supplementation to prevent hypoglycemia. High-protein diet (3 g/kg) is recommended to provide amino acid substrate for gluconeogenesis and to support muscle integrity. Uncooked cornstarch provides a slow-release glucose source. Modified Atkins or ketogenic diets may improve cardiac and skeletal muscle function by reducing glycogen storage.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type III.
evidence.snippet: Dietary management tailored to the individual patient remains the primary therapy. Frequent feeds (every 3-4 hours) are needed to maintain euglycemia in infancy.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy of cornstarch therapy in type III glycogen-storage disease.
evidence.snippet: Cornstarch therapy was associated with maintenance of normoglycemia, increased growth velocity, and decreased serum aminotransferase concentrations in all patients.

evidence.reference_title: Improvement of Cardiomyopathy After High-Fat Diet in Two Siblings with Glycogen Storage Disease Type III.
evidence.snippet: A diet rich in fats as well as proteins and poor in carbohydrates could be a beneficial therapeutic choice for GSD III with cardiomyopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Markers of liver injury (aminotransferases), dysfunction (cholesterol, triglycerides), and glycogen storage (glucose tetrasaccharide, Glc4) were elevated at an early age, and decreased significantly thereafter

readouts.interpretation: Higher cholesterol tracks hepatic metabolic dysfunction in GSD III.

nodes.description: Elevated CK levels reflecting skeletal muscle involvement, frequently found in GSD IIIa patients. CK is significantly associated with disease burden.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Creatine phosphokinase was also elevated with no significant correlation with age (p = 0.4).

nodes.description: Elevated liver transaminases (AST and ALT) reflecting hepatocyte injury from glycogen accumulation. Tend to normalize with age as fibrosis progresses.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Markers of liver injury (aminotransferases), dysfunction (cholesterol, triglycerides), and glycogen storage (glucose tetrasaccharide, Glc4) were elevated at an early age, and decreased significantly thereafter

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Markers of liver injury (aminotransferases), dysfunction (cholesterol, triglycerides), and glycogen storage (glucose tetrasaccharide, Glc4) were elevated at an early age, and decreased significantly thereafter

readouts.interpretation: Higher Glc4 reports glycogen storage burden in the hepatic branch.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Markers of liver injury (aminotransferases), dysfunction (cholesterol, triglycerides), and glycogen storage (glucose tetrasaccharide, Glc4) were elevated at an early age, and decreased significantly thereafter

readouts.interpretation: Higher triglycerides track hepatic metabolic dysfunction in GSD III.

nodes.description: Growth retardation and short stature, particularly in childhood, due to metabolic derangements and recurrent hypoglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type III.
evidence.snippet: In infancy and early childhood, liver involvement presents as hepatomegaly and failure to thrive

nodes.description: Ketotic hypoglycemia during fasting due to impaired glycogenolysis. Less severe than in GSD I because gluconeogenesis is intact. More than half of patients report hospitalizations due to hypoglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type III.
evidence.snippet: In infancy and early childhood, liver involvement presents as hepatomegaly and failure to thrive, with fasting ketotic hypoglycemia, hyperlipidemia, and elevated hepatic transaminases.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: a mixed-methods study to assess the burden of disease.
evidence.snippet: Hypoglycemia was frequently reported in both adults and children, with more than half reporting hospitalizations due to hypoglycemia.

nodes.description: Deficiency of the glycogen debranching enzyme (AGL) impairs the complete degradation of glycogen. The enzyme normally has two catalytic activities: oligo-1,4-1,4-glucantransferase (transferase) and amylo-1,6-glucosidase (glucosidase). Without this enzyme, glycogen breakdown halts at the branch points, leading to accumulation of limit dextrin (abnormally short-branched glycogen) in hepatocytes, skeletal myocytes, and cardiomyocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular characterization of glycogen storage disease type III.
evidence.snippet: Most GSD-III patients have AGL deficiency in both the liver and muscle (type IIIa), but some have it in the liver but not muscle (type IIIb).

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: diagnosis, genotype, management, clinical course and outcome.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen storage disease type III (GSDIII) is a rare disorder of glycogenolysis due to AGL gene mutations, causing glycogen debranching enzyme deficiency and storage of limited dextrin.

evidence.reference_title: Narrative review of glycogen storage disorder type III with a focus on neuromuscular, cardiac and therapeutic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen storage disorder type III (GSDIII) is a rare inborn error of metabolism due to loss of glycogen debranching enzyme activity, causing inability to fully mobilize glycogen stores and its consequent accumulation in various tissues, notably liver, cardiac and skeletal muscle.

nodes.description: Progressive hepatic fibrosis develops in many patients, potentially leading to cirrhosis in adulthood. Fibrosis can be present from infancy and may be severe even in young children.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Liver fibrosis can occur at an early age, and may explain the decrease in aminotransferases and Glc4 with age.

evidence.reference_title: A retrospective longitudinal study and comprehensive review of adult patients with glycogen storage disease type III.
evidence.snippet: 28% developed decompensated liver disease and portal hypertension

nodes.description: Progressive accumulation of limit dextrin in hepatocytes causes hepatomegaly and hepatic dysfunction. Over time, the glycogen storage leads to hepatocyte injury, inflammation, fibrosis, and potentially cirrhosis. Hepatocellular adenomas and carcinoma can develop as long-term complications. Liver fibrosis can begin early in life; aminotransferases may paradoxically normalize with age as fibrosis progresses.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Liver fibrosis can occur at an early age, and may explain the decrease in aminotransferases and Glc4 with age.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: diagnosis, genotype, management, clinical course and outcome.
evidence.snippet: Chronic complications involved the liver (hepatic cirrhosis, adenoma(s), and/or hepatocellular carcinoma in 11 %)

evidence.reference_title: A retrospective longitudinal study and comprehensive review of adult patients with glycogen storage disease type III.
evidence.snippet: hepatomegaly and cirrhosis were the most common radiological findings; and 28% developed decompensated liver disease and portal hypertension, the latter being more prevalent in older patients.

nodes.description: Enlargement of the liver due to glycogen accumulation, present from infancy. Typically most prominent in childhood and may decrease with age. Hepatomegaly is the most common presenting sign.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: diagnosis, genotype, management, clinical course and outcome.
evidence.snippet: GSDIII patients first presented before the age of 1.5 years, hepatomegaly was the most common presenting clinical sign.

nodes.description: Left ventricular hypertrophy is common in GSD IIIa patients and may be present from childhood. Asymptomatic LVH is the most common cardiac manifestation. Symptomatic cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction occurs in approximately 10-15% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: A retrospective longitudinal study and comprehensive review of adult patients with glycogen storage disease type III.
evidence.snippet: Asymptomatic left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) was the most common cardiac manifestation, present in 43%. Symptomatic cardiomyopathy and reduced ejection fraction was evident in 10%.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: diagnosis, genotype, management, clinical course and outcome.
evidence.snippet: heart (cardiac involvement and cardiomyopathy, in 58 % and 15 %, respectively, generally presenting in early childhood)

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type III.
evidence.snippet: In infancy and early childhood, liver involvement presents as hepatomegaly and failure to thrive, with fasting ketotic hypoglycemia, hyperlipidemia, and elevated hepatic transaminases.

readouts.interpretation: Lower blood glucose reports impaired hepatic glycogen mobilization during fasting.
Blood glucose monitoring tracks the fasting hypoglycemia branch.

nodes.description: Progressive skeletal myopathy in GSD IIIa, manifesting as proximal muscle weakness and exercise intolerance. Distal myopathy may also develop. Muscle weakness is the major cause of morbidity in adult GSD IIIa patients.

evidence.reference_title: A retrospective longitudinal study and comprehensive review of adult patients with glycogen storage disease type III.
evidence.snippet: In the GSD IIIa group, muscle weakness was a major feature, noted in 89% of the GSD IIIa cohort, a third of whom depended on a wheelchair or an assistive walking device.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: a mixed-methods study to assess the burden of disease.
evidence.snippet: Adults most often reported muscle weakness as a top interfering symptom and the most important goal of a potential therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis during clinical ascertainment of glycogen storage disease type III: a need for improved and systematic monitoring.
evidence.snippet: Creatine phosphokinase was also elevated with no significant correlation with age (p = 0.4).

readouts.interpretation: Higher serum creatine kinase reports the myopathy branch of GSD III.

nodes.description: In GSD IIIa, glycogen accumulation in skeletal and cardiac muscle leads to progressive myopathy and cardiomyopathy. Muscle involvement may be minimal in childhood but typically worsens with age. The myopathy is slowly progressive and can lead to significant disability. Cardiac involvement manifests primarily as left ventricular hypertrophy, which may progress to cardiomyopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type III.
evidence.snippet: Most individuals develop cardiac involvement with cardiac hypertrophy and/or cardiomyopathy. Skeletal myopathy manifesting as weakness may be evident in childhood and slowly progresses, typically becoming prominent in the third to fourth decade.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type III: diagnosis, genotype, management, clinical course and outcome.
evidence.snippet: heart (cardiac involvement and cardiomyopathy, in 58 % and 15 %, respectively, generally presenting in early childhood), and muscle (pain in 34 %).

evidence.reference_title: A retrospective longitudinal study and comprehensive review of adult patients with glycogen storage disease type III.
evidence.snippet: muscle weakness was a major feature, noted in 89% of the GSD IIIa cohort, a third of whom depended on a wheelchair or an assistive walking device.
